title: Mindfulness Definitions

pages.text:
46
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Mindfulness Handout 1a
Mindfulness Definitions
What Is Mindfulness?
•• Intentionally living with awareness in the present moment.
(Waking up from automatic or rote behaviors to participate and be present to our own lives.)
•• Without judging or rejecting the moment.
(Noticing consequences, discerning helpfulness and harmfulness—but letting go of evaluating,
avoiding, suppressing, or blocking the present moment.)
•• Without attachment to the moment.
(Attending to the experience of each new moment, rather than ignoring the present by clinging
to the past or grabbing for the future.)
What Are Mindfulness Skills?
•• Mindfulness skills are the specific behaviors to practice that, when put together, make up
mindfulness.
What Is Mindfulness Practice?
•• Mindfulness and mindfulness skills can be practiced at any time, anywhere, while doing
anything. Intentionally paying attention to the moment, without judging it or holding on to it, is all
that is needed.
•• Meditation is practicing mindfulness and mindfulness skills while sitting, standing, or lying
quietly for a predetermined period of time. When meditating, we focus the mind (for example,
we focus on body sensations, emotions, thoughts, or our breath), or we open the mind (paying
attention to whatever comes into our awareness). There are many forms of meditation that differ
mostly by whether we are opening the mind or focusing the mind—and, if focusing, depending
on what is the focus of our attention.
•• Contemplative prayer (such as Christian centering prayer, the rosary, Jewish Shema, Islamic
Sufi practice, or Hindu raja yoga) is a spiritual mindfulness practice.
•• Mindfulness movement also has many forms. Examples include yoga, martial arts (such as
Qigong, tai chi, akido, and karate), and spiritual dancing. Hiking, horseback riding, and walking
can also be ways to practice mindfulness.

Mindfulness Handout 1a
Mindfulness Definitions
What Is Mindfulness?
•• Intentionally living with awareness in the present moment.
(Waking up from automatic or rote behaviors to participate and be present to our own lives.)
•• Without judging or rejecting the moment.
(Noticing consequences, discerning helpfulness and harmfulness—but letting go of evaluating,
avoiding, suppressing, or blocking the present moment.)
•• Without attachment to the moment.
(Attending to the experience of each new moment, rather than ignoring the present by clinging
to the past or grabbing for the future.)
What Are Mindfulness Skills?
•• Mindfulness skills are the specific behaviors to practice that, when put together, make up
mindfulness.
What Is Mindfulness Practice?
•• Mindfulness and mindfulness skills can be practiced at any time, anywhere, while doing
anything. Intentionally paying attention to the moment, without judging it or holding on to it, is all

•• Meditation is practicing mindfulness and mindfulness skills while sitting, standing, or lying

we focus on body sensations, emotions, thoughts, or our breath), or we open the mind (paying

•• Contemplative prayer (such as Christian centering prayer, the rosary, Jewish Shema, Islamic

•• Mindfulness movement also has many forms. Examples include yoga, martial arts (such as

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Mindfulness Definitions
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Mindfulness is allowed to be tiny, sensory-safe, and done from bed or wheelchair. Noticing without judging counts even when the day is messy.
tiny_steps: Find one neutral thing in the room.
Return to the next safe action.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use eyes-open grounding if closing eyes feels unsafe.
Use quiet, low-scent, low-touch options for MCAS, trauma, and sensory overload.
No posture requirement; lying down or supported sitting is valid.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not force stillness when it increases pain, panic, dysphoria, or trauma activation.

ourspace_shared_revision_metadata.copyright_note: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.
